course_title: Introduction to Philosophy: What is Philosophy?

learning_outcomes.description: Understand some of the central debates in philosophy (breadth and depth)
Demonstrate basic skills of effective thinking and communication (clarity, relevance, accuracy)
Conduct independent philosophical research and inquiry
Identify, analyse and evaluate arguments or points of view
Understand the role of philosophy in everyday life

assessments.task_description:
There will be short, in-class, closed-book quizzes in the tutorials. The questions could include multiple-choice, true-false, or short answer. They are designed to test the extent to which students are keeping up with and understanding lecture content and set readings. More detailed advice will be available in the "Assessment" folder on the Blackboard online teaching page regarding preparation for these quizzes and the content they cover. Tutorials run during weeks 2-4, and weeks 6-12 and are face-to-face. This is an in-person assessment not permitting use of notes or AI. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

Please see assessment criteria below under "Additional Course Grading information".
assessments.submission_guidelines: Quizzes will be conducted in class on paper.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

assessments.task_description: Short Answer Test conducted during the lecture on 27 August, via Blackboard. Students may take the exam on their own laptop or by organising a laptop on loan from the UQ library ahead of time. Exam is based on content in first four weeks. Please see assessment criteria below under "Additional Course Grading information". In addition, further guidance about preparation for this assessment item will be added to the Blackboard Assessment folder.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Exam is timed. Submit via Blackboard during the lecture period 27 August at the end of the 60 minutes.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

assessments.task_description:
Students will be required to write a philosophical essay on an assigned question related to the course. Questions will be specified in the ‘Essay Task Sheet' on the Blackboard site under the 'Assessment.' folder. Word count: 1600 words (+/- 10%). A word count that is within ±10% of the set length (word limit) is acceptable. Word count does not include footnotes, citations, titles or reference list; however, discursive footnotes are included in the word count. A word count that is outside these 10% will receive a proportionate penalty and will be graded against the grading criteria. Although we encourage Chicago style, for this assignment you may use APA , MLA or Chicago referencing styles as described in the UQ Library Referencing Guides, provided students provide page numbers or sections where points occur in sources referenced, and also reference and/or quote ideas or wording arising from AI sources. Assignments must be submitted via Turnitin (on Blackboard); check that your submission has been received.

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. Use of AI for "ideas" or any points not arising from the student's own thinking will need to referenced in accordance with university policy in the manner illustrated in the UQ Library Referencing Guidelines for the reference style chosen by the student. Any direct quotation from AI will need to be both referenced and in direct quotation.

Please see assessment criteria below under "Additional Course Grading information".

Please see Blackboard “Assessment” folder for more detailed guidance about the essay.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description: The final exam will occur during the final exam block, and will require students to display understanding and critical analysis of one or more works learned during the semester, and/or in response to a question or stimulus. The exam will be closed-book. Students may take the exam on their own laptop or by organising a laptop on loan from the UQ library ahead of time. Additional guidance about the structure and how to prepare for the exam will be provided in the "Assessment" folder in Blackboard.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Exams are submitted via the Inspera Exam Platform.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.
